races.name: Tour of the Alps – Stage 1
races.finish_type: Flat sprint / reduced group after punchy climbs
races.description: The 49th Tour of the Alps — a key Giro d'Italia build-up race — opens with 144.3km in and around Innsbruck, starting and finishing on the Rennweg (the same finish used at the 2018 World Championships road race). The race is rated the easiest stage of the five, but the circuit profile should still produce a selective finish. After a flat approach through the Inn Valley, the riders tackle two loops: the first (completed twice) includes the Mieminger Plateau (6.2km @ 3.7%, KOM points on 2nd pass only); the second (18km, also done twice) features the decisive Götzens climb (4km @ 7%, with sections in double digits), followed by a drag to Axams (1km @ 5.9%), where time bonuses are offered at the intermediate sprint on the 2nd passage (6s/4s/2s). After the 2nd Axams passage, the riders descend 6km to the Inn Valley and race 17km flat to the Rennweg finish. Time bonuses: 10s/6s/4s at finish; 6s/4s/2s at Axams intermediate sprint. The stage could finish in a sprint from a reduced group if no strong moves go on Götzens, but the terrain is hard enough to crack the pure sprinters. A 10th anniversary edition: the transnational Euregio Tirol, Südtirol/Alto Adige, and Trentino agreement was signed in 2016, giving birth to the modern Tour of the Alps.

stages.notes: Circuit race with Götzens (4km @ 7%) x2. Flat finish. Today.
Summit finish at Martell (6km @ 8% avg). First major mountain test. Pillerhöhe (9.2km @ 6%) 100+km out.
Crossing into Trentino. Contains major climb then descent. Same day as La Flèche Wallonne.
Short punchy climb to Bovo with time bonuses inside final 10km. Race-defining stage.
Final stage. Circuit with Cat.1 Nobls/Montoppio (12.7km @ 7%) x2. Fast finish in Bolzano.

key_climbs.name: Götzens
key_climbs.notes: Double-digit gradients in 3rd km. Decisive selection climb.

races.rider_notes: Four pre-race GC favourites identified at the official press conference: Michael Storer (Tudor, defending champion), Egan Bernal (INEOS, comeback season), Tom Pidcock (Pinarello-Q36.5, return from Volta a Catalunya crash), Giulio Pellizzari (Red Bull-Bora, Italian U23 talent, GC favourite). For Stage 1 specifically: Pidcock is the top favourite given the punchy profile and sprint from a small group — though fitness post-crash is uncertain. Pellizzari also suits the profile. Victor Langellotti (INEOS) is a sprint threat in small groups. Matteo Sobrero (Lidl-Trek) is fast enough if the stage comes down to a reduced bunch. Lorenzo Finn (Red Bull, U23 Road World Champion) could surprise. Breakaway also viable on this opening stage. Notable absences: No Tadej Pogačar, no Jonas Vingegaard, no Mathieu van der Poel — this is a Giro build-up race for climbers. Isaac Del Toro's Ardennes plans are uncertain after Itzulia crash. Ben Healy (EF) has a broken sacrum from Itzulia crash, not racing.

broadcasts.notes: Live from ~13:30 CEST (11:30 UTC) on TNT Sports and HBO Max. Exact window may start later given 15:15 finish. Plans from £25.99/month.
Live worldwide from 13:30 CEST (11:30 UTC) per official race site. Eurosport and HBO Max.
RAI Sport typically broadcasts the Tour of the Alps live in Italy for free.
ORF may broadcast the Austrian stage. Eurosport also available.

data_confidence.notes: 49th edition confirmed (race started 1963, renamed from Giro del Trentino in 2016; 2026 = 49th running). Dates April 20-24 confirmed by official site, Cyclingnews, cyclingstage.com. Stage 1 Innsbruck → Innsbruck, 144.3km, 1,901m confirmed by PCS stage profile page (start time 11:55 CEST per PCS; 11:45 CEST per cyclingstage.com route page; official site says live from 13:30 CEST which is ~mid-race coverage, not start). Using 11:45 CEST as start time per cyclingstage.com. Götzens climb 4km @ 7% with double-digit sections confirmed by multiple preview sources. Axams time bonuses 6s/4s/2s on 2nd passage confirmed by cyclingstage.com route guide. Finish time bonuses 10s/6s/4s confirmed. Worldwide live broadcast from 13:30 CEST confirmed by official race site. UK on TNT Sports/HBO Max, US/CA on FloBikes, EU on Eurosport/HBO Max all confirmed. RAI Sport (Italy) and ORF (Austria) are likely but not confirmed from search results.

recent_results.notes: Evenepoel took revenge for his 3rd place in 2025, outsprinting Skjelmose in a tense two-man finale after the same decisive selection point as last year. 'I felt much better than last year — my sprint was much better, I had something left.' Race made in the same place as 2025. Red Bull-Bora did huge team work, chasing down the break from 100km out. Absentees: Del Toro and Ayuso both ill; Pidcock chose Tour of the Alps; Healy (broken sacrum from Itzulia); Ben Tulett (unfit, DNS morning of race).

recent_results.race: Amstel Gold Race Ladies Edition (1.WWT, Netherlands)
recent_results.notes: Blasi, 23, Spanish, launched a decisive attack on the penultimate Cauberg ascent, went solo and held off the chasers for a shock win — biggest result of her career. Vollering (FDJ, defending winner of Flanders and Paris-Roubaix) could not continue her team's dominant spring. Niewiadoma-Phinney again denied at a major classic.

recent_results.race: Grand Prix Féminin de Chambéry (WE 1.1, France)
recent_results.notes: Gery won her second race in two days — also won Brabantse Pijl Women on April 17. The 20-year-old Frenchwoman (also a cyclocross racer) launched a late flyer on the uphill finish in Chambéry-le-Vieux to win the 23rd edition of this 1.1 race (122.1km). First manche of the Coupe de France Femmes Professionnelles FDJ United.

recent_results.race: O Gran Camiño – Stage 5 / Overall (2.1, Spain)
recent_results.notes: Adam Yates sealed the overall O Gran Camiño victory after taking command with his Stage 4 summit finish win. Pinarello won the final stage atop Monte Trega. Yates, using the race as Giro d'Italia preparation, took his first win of 2026.

Foldager stunned the field with a sprint victory — a surprise win for the young Dane in the field's finishing sprint after the repeated Overijse circuit. Evenepoel did race (contradicting some pre-race uncertainty) and finished. Pidcock withdrew.
Gery's breakthrough first pro win — 20-year-old Frenchwoman won in a small group sprint in Overijse. Two days later won GP Féminin de Chambéry for a stunning double.

notes: Remco Evenepoel's Amstel Gold Race win confirms he is at his best for the Ardennes. La Flèche Wallonne (Wednesday, April 22) and Liège-Bastogne-Liège (Sunday, April 26) await.
Tom Pidcock skipped Amstel Gold Race and chose Tour of the Alps instead — targeting Liège-Bastogne-Liège. This is his return from the Volta a Catalunya ravine crash in March.
Célia Gery (FDJ United-SUEZ, 20) is the breakout star of the week: Brabantse Pijl Women + GP Féminin de Chambéry in 48 hours. A genuine French cycling talent to watch.
The Tour of the Alps is the final major stage race before La Flèche Wallonne (April 22) and Liège-Bastogne-Liège (April 26). Its field is packed with Giro d'Italia contenders.
Notable DNS/non-starters at Amstel: Del Toro, Ayuso (illness), Healy (broken sacrum), Tulett (unfit morning of race), Pidcock (chose Tour of Alps).
Matteo Jorgenson (Visma) suffered a broken collarbone — end of his Ardennes season confirmed.

upcoming.notes: Telfs → Martell/Val Martello, 147.5km. First proper summit finish: Martell 6km @ 8% avg. True GC day — Pillerhöhe (9.2km @ 6%) over 100km from finish precedes the key ascent.
The Mur de Huy specialist race. Evenepoel targeting win after Amstel. Pogačar not expected to race. Vollering favourite for women. Women's race (La Flèche Wallonne Féminine) same day.
Mur de Huy. Vollering, Koch, Wiebes the likely key names.
La Doyenne — the final Monument of the spring. 260km+ through the Belgian Ardennes. Evenepoel hunting the win. Also: Liège-Bastogne-Liège Femmes same weekend.

issues: Tour of the Alps stage start time: CyclingUpToDate preview says 11:55 CEST; cyclingstage.com route page says start 11:45 CEST; official race site says 'live worldwide from 13:30 CEST' (this is the broadcast start, not the race start). Using 11:45 CEST per cyclingstage.com. The official site figure is for broadcasters, not the rolling start.
Italy/Austria free broadcasts: RAI Sport and ORF are standard broadcasters for the Tour of the Alps historically but not confirmed from search results today. Flagged as 'likely' based on precedent.

O Gran Camiño Stage 5 winner: Stage 5 winner listed as Alessandro Pinarello from the Cyclingnews calendar list ('O Gran Camiño: Adam Yates seals overall victory as Pinarello wins atop Monte Trega'). This is treated as confirmed.

Evenepoel at Brabantse Pijl: He did race (per Cyclingnews live coverage 'the race more or less opened in the same place this year again' — and Evenepoel was quoted post-race about Amstel, suggesting he had done both races). Foldager won the men's Brabantse Pijl per Cyclingnews calendar entry.

No other 1.1 or 2.1 races found for April 20 beyond Tour of the Alps Stage 1. PCS 'today' calendar couldn't be fetched (403 error) but cross-referenced against known calendar sources. April 20 (Monday) is typically a rest day between Amstel and Flèche Wallonne, with Tour of the Alps the sole significant action.
